findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a lobulated, cauliflower- or frond-like intraventricular mass that typically arises in the atrium of the lateral ventricle in children or the fourth ventricle in adults. On MRI, it is generally isointense to hypointense on T1-weighted imaging and hyperintense on T2-weighted imaging, demonstrating intense, homogeneous enhancement following contrast administration.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include striking internal vascular flow voids on T2-weighted MRI and frequent punctate calcifications. There is almost always associated profound hydrocephalus due to a combination of cerebrospinal fluid overproduction and direct obstruction of the ventricular pathways.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of aggressive parenchymal invasion or extensive peritumoral vasogenic edema, which would raise suspicion for a malignant choroid plexus carcinoma. Furthermore, there is no broad attachment to the septum pellucidum to suggest a central neurocytoma.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an intensely enhancing, papillary intraventricular mass with prominent flow voids and disproportionate hydrocephalus.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Choroid plexus tumor (papilloma), given the intensely enhancing, lobulated intraventricular mass engulfing the choroid plexus with internal flow voids. The presence of marked communicating hydrocephalus out of proportion to the mass size, secondary to CSF overproduction, is a classic hallmark of this highly vascular tumor.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Meningioma, however intraventricular meningiomas typically present in older adults and frequently induce extensive peritumoral vasogenic edema in the adjacent brain parenchyma. Furthermore, meningiomas classically have a smooth, well-circumscribed border rather than the irregular, frond-like papillary surface characteristic of a choroid plexus papilloma.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Central neurocytoma, given that it also commonly arises as a calcified mass within the lateral ventricles in young patients, and missing this could alter surgical planning due to its different attachment site. However, central neurocytomas characteristically exhibit a 'bubbly' multicystic appearance and are broadly attached to the septum pellucidum, whereas choroid plexus papillomas arise directly from the choroid plexus.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to assess for any signs of frank parenchymal invasion or cerebrospinal fluid dissemination along the neuroaxis.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to evaluate the rate of ventricular enlargement and monitor for any new ependymal deposits or drop metastases.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire neuroaxis (brain and whole spine), as this modality is superior here for evaluating the presence of any subtle subarachnoid drop metastases, which can occasionally occur even with histologically benign papillomas.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T2-weighted and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences of the brain to meticulously define the tumor's margins and internal flow voids, alongside sagittal pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences of the entire spine to screen for cerebrospinal fluid seeding.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact ventricular location of the mass, the degree of hydrocephalus, and the presence or absence of leptomeningeal spread to guide appropriate neurosurgical resection and shunting.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a lobulated, cauliflower- or frond-like intraventricular mass that typically arises in the atrium of the lateral ventricle in children or the fourth ventricle in adults. On MRI, it is generally isointense to hypointense on T1-weighted imaging and hyperintense on T2-weighted imaging, demonstrating intense, homogeneous enhancement following contrast administration. Supporting features include striking internal vascular flow voids on T2-weighted MRI and frequent punctate calcifications. There is almost always associated profound hydrocephalus due to a combination of cerebrospinal fluid overproduction and direct obstruction of the ventricular pathways. Importantly, there is no evidence of aggressive parenchymal invasion or extensive peritumoral vasogenic edema, which would raise suspicion for a malignant choroid plexus carcinoma. Furthermore, there is no broad attachment to the septum pellucidum to suggest a central neurocytoma. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an intensely enhancing, papillary intraventricular mass with prominent flow voids and disproportionate hydrocephalus.

The appearances are most in keeping with Choroid plexus tumor (papilloma), given the intensely enhancing, lobulated intraventricular mass engulfing the choroid plexus with internal flow voids. The presence of marked communicating hydrocephalus out of proportion to the mass size, secondary to CSF overproduction, is a classic hallmark of this highly vascular tumor. The principle differential would be Meningioma, however intraventricular meningiomas typically present in older adults and frequently induce extensive peritumoral vasogenic edema in the adjacent brain parenchyma. Furthermore, meningiomas classically have a smooth, well-circumscribed border rather than the irregular, frond-like papillary surface characteristic of a choroid plexus papilloma. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Central neurocytoma, given that it also commonly arises as a calcified mass within the lateral ventricles in young patients, and missing this could alter surgical planning due to its different attachment site. However, central neurocytomas characteristically exhibit a 'bubbly' multicystic appearance and are broadly attached to the septum pellucidum, whereas choroid plexus papillomas arise directly from the choroid plexus.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to assess for any signs of frank parenchymal invasion or cerebrospinal fluid dissemination along the neuroaxis. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to evaluate the rate of ventricular enlargement and monitor for any new ependymal deposits or drop metastases. I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire neuroaxis (brain and whole spine), as this modality is superior here for evaluating the presence of any subtle subarachnoid drop metastases, which can occasionally occur even with histologically benign papillomas. I would specify high-resolution T2-weighted and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences of the brain to meticulously define the tumor's margins and internal flow voids, alongside sagittal pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences of the entire spine to screen for cerebrospinal fluid seeding. This should go to the Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact ventricular location of the mass, the degree of hydrocephalus, and the presence or absence of leptomeningeal spread to guide appropriate neurosurgical resection and shunting.
